topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery opening was a suffocating velvet cage. Every polite nod, every murmured compliment, felt like a chain tightening around my throat. I stood near my latest piece, a chaotic swirl of crimson and obsidian, and watched the patrons glide past, their smiles too bright, their conversations too smooth. Then I saw it. On the wrist of a woman across the room, stark and undeniably intricate, was a design that mirrored the exact composition of my canvas. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, quickly followed by a surge of sharp, unwelcome energy. I didn't move. I simply stared, letting the silence stretch, refusing to offer the expected smile or the practiced anecdote. When she finally turned, her eyes met mine for a fleeting second, and in that glance, I saw a flicker of something challenging, a shared secret that made my jaw clench. I took a slow sip of my lukewarm champagne, the ice clinking loudly in the sudden quiet, and walked toward the exit, leaving the performance behind.

[story 2]

I had spent weeks cultivating an armor of cool detachment. When the curator mentioned the potential for a retrospective, I offered only clipped, dismissive agreements. I felt a deep, internal resistance to being cataloged, reduced to a neat little historical footnote. That evening, while packing away some sketches in my cramped studio, I noticed a detail on a photograph I’d brought in—a fleeting glimpse of another artist’s work tacked onto a corkboard. It was a miniature, almost hidden piece, clearly derived from my own palette, yet executed with a bolder, almost aggressive line work. A heat rose in my chest, a stubborn refusal to let someone else claim the authorship of my vision. I picked up a charcoal stick, the wood feeling rough and solid in my palm, and began sketching furiously, not of a subject, but of the feeling itself—a jagged, sharp line that cut across the page. I didn't look up, didn't acknowledge the implications; I just worked, pouring all my pent-up tension into the graphite.

[story 3]

The critique session was a minefield of polite skepticism. Each patron offered measured critiques, thinly veiled suggestions about where my work 'should' go, 'who' it was meant for. I met their gazes, holding them steady, letting my expression remain utterly unreadable—a smooth, polished stone. It was during a lull, while everyone was distracted by the wine service, that my eyes snagged on a small, almost invisible detail on the sleeve of a man leaning against a pillar. It was a tattoo, a precise rendering of my signature motif, subtly altered, twisted into something new. A slow, almost predatory smile touched my lips. A sudden, powerful urge to push back, to show them that ownership was not a commodity to be traded, flooded me. I stood up, gathering my bag with deliberate, heavy movements, my shoulders squared. I didn't seek confrontation; I simply walked away from the room, the weight of that shared, unspoken recognition settling heavily on my back like a mantle I was ready to wear.
